subject.name: Health & Safety
subject.description: PPE, risk assessment, method statements, CDM and site safety law.

quizzes.title: PPE Identification & Selection
quizzes.description: Pick the right kit for the job — and know why.

questions.text: Under the PPE at Work Regulations, who must pay for PPE required for work?
options: The employer, at no cost to the worker
questions.explain: PPE must be provided free of charge. Charging a worker for required PPE is unlawful.

questions.text: PPE sits where in the hierarchy of control?
options: Last — a final resort once other controls are applied
It is not part of the hierarchy
questions.explain: Eliminate → substitute → engineering controls → administrative controls → PPE. PPE only protects the wearer and only if used correctly.

questions.text: Select ALL PPE required for cutting concrete blocks with a disc cutter outdoors.
questions.explain: Respirable crystalline silica makes an FFP3 mandatory. Dielectric (electrical) gloves are irrelevant here.

questions.text: Match each PPE item to the hazard it controls.

pairs.right: Lacerations from blades and sharp edges

questions.explain: Each control targets a specific hazard — generic 'wear PPE' is not a risk control.

questions.text: A hard hat has no expiry and can be used indefinitely if it looks undamaged.
questions.explain: Shells degrade under UV. Manufacturers typically specify replacement 2–5 years from first use, and immediately after any impact.

quizzes.title: Risk Assessment & Method Statements
quizzes.description: The five steps, RAMS, and what a decent control actually looks like.

questions.text: Put the five steps of risk assessment into the correct order.
items: Decide who might be harmed and how
Evaluate the risks and decide on control measures
Record your findings and implement them
Review the assessment and update if necessary
questions.explain: HSE's five steps. Review is not optional — it is triggered by any change to the work, the people or the site.

questions.text: What is the difference between a hazard and a risk?
options: A hazard is something with the potential to cause harm; risk is the likelihood and severity of that harm occurring
A hazard is minor; a risk is serious
A risk is a legal term; a hazard is not
questions.explain: A trailing lead is a hazard. The chance of someone tripping over it and how badly they'd be hurt is the risk.

questions.text: A method statement should be written by whom?
options: A competent person with knowledge of the task and its hazards
questions.explain: Competence means the training, knowledge and experience to do it properly — not just a job title.

questions.scenario: You are due to install pipework at 2.8 m in an occupied office. The RAMS specifies a podium step. On arrival the podium step is missing and only a wooden pallet stacked on a desk is available. The client is pressing you to finish today.
options: Use the pallet — it's only a short job
Stop work and do not proceed until the correct access equipment is available
Ask a colleague to foot the pallet
Work from the desk without the pallet
questions.explain: Work at height must follow the RAMS. Improvising access is exactly how the majority of fatal construction falls happen. Commercial pressure is never a defence.

questions.text: Under RIDDOR, within what period must a specified injury be reported to the HSE?
options: Immediately, then in writing within 10 days
Only at the end of the project
questions.explain: Specified injuries must be reported without delay, with the written report following within 10 days. Over-7-day injuries get 15 days.

questions.text: Drag each control into the correct level of the hierarchy of control.

items.label: Pre-fabricate the assembly at ground level
Use a low-solvent adhesive instead of a solvent-based one
Fit on-tool dust extraction to the cutter
Rotate staff to limit exposure time

questions.explain: Work down the list, not up it. Reaching for PPE first is the most common failure in a weak risk assessment.

quizzes.title: Legislation & CDM
quizzes.description: Who is responsible for what, and under which Act.

questions.text: Which Act places a general duty on employers to ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, the health and safety of employees?
options: Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974
questions.explain: Section 2 of HASAWA 1974. Section 7 places the matching duty on employees to take reasonable care.

questions.text: Under CDM 2015, who has overall control of the construction phase on site?
questions.explain: The principal designer controls the pre-construction phase; the principal contractor controls the construction phase.

questions.text: Under HASAWA section 7, an employee can be prosecuted for failing to take reasonable care of their own safety.
questions.explain: Duties run both ways. Employees have been prosecuted for removing guards or ignoring safe systems of work.

questions.text: Match each regulation to its subject.
questions.explain: Learn the acronyms — they come up constantly in exams and on site inductions.
